coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photoluminescence property and broad color emission of ZnGa2O4 phosphor due to the synergistic role of Eu3+ and carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Huo, Qiuyue
Tu, Weixia
Guo, Lin
coredata.dc:description: ZnGa2O4 phosphors co-composited with nanoscale carbon dots (CDs) and Eu3+ were presented for the tunable color emission. Novel single phase CDs or/and Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors were synthesized by microwave hydrothermal method and their optical properties were investigated. The ZnGa2O4 phosphors composited with CDs exhibited an intense broad blue light emission at 421 nm and a more enhanced photoluminescence intensity than those without CDs. The Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors gave an ideal red color emission. The CDs/Eu3+ co-composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors exhibited a wide emission band peak at 450 nm and narrow emission peak at 618 nm. Furthermore, the tunable color emissions of CDs/Eu3+ co-composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors from blue to the white light region, and then to red were obtained with the increasing Eu3+ concentration, which can be a promising single phased phosphor candidate in light emitting diodes. Broadly tunable emission single phased phosphor is tuned firstly through the synergistic role of the non-metal element and the rare earth metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Zinc gallate
Luminescence
Phosphors
Lanthanides

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs or/and Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors
Synthesis and characterization of ZnGa2O4 phosphors composited with CDs or/and Eu3+
Photoluminescence properties of the CDs-composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors
Luminescence properties of the Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors
Photoluminescence properties of the Eu3+/CDs composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of ZnGa2O4 (a), ZnGa2O4:4%CDs (b), ZnGa2O4:8%Eu3+ (c) and ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,8% Eu3+ (d) nanoparticles.
XRD patterns of ZnGa2O4 (a), ZnGa2O4:4%CDs (b), ZnGa2O4:8%Eu3+(c) and ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,8%Eu3+ (d).
XPS spectra of ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,8%Eu3+ with Ga 2p and Eu 3d (a), Zn 2p (b) and C 1s (c) binding energy, and FTIR spectrum (d) of ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,8%Eu3+ phosphor.
Excitation spectra (a) and the emission spectra (b) of ZnGa2O4:xCDs (x = 0, 1.4, 4.0, 6.5 and 8.9 mol%) phosphors.

Excitation spectra (a) and emission spectra (b) of ZnGa2O4:yEu3+phosphors, concentration dependence of the emission intensity of Eu3+ 5D0 -7F2 transitions in ZnGa2O4:yEu3+ (y = 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 and 11 mol%) (c) and the linear relationship between log(I/m) and log(m) in the case of y = 8, 9 and 11 mol% (d).
PL spectra of ZnGa2O4:4%CDs (a) and ZnGa1.98O4:8% Eu3+ (b).
Excitation spectra (a) and emission spectra (b) of ZnGa2O4:4%CDs, yEu3+(y = 1, 3, 6, 7, 8, 9 and 11 mol%) phosphors.
PL spectra of the physical mixtures including CDs + ZnGa2O4 (a), CDs + Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 (b), Eu3+ + CDs composited ZnGa2O4 (c) and Eu3+ + ZnGa2O4 (d).
Photoluminescence decay curves of ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,y%Eu3+ (y = 7, 8, 9 and 11 mol%) phosphors (λex = 395 nm).

The CIE chromaticity coordinates of the ZnGa2O4:4%CDs (cross 1), ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,yEu3+(y = 1 (cross A), 3 (cross B), 8 (cross C) and 11 mol% (cross D)), and ZnGa2O4:8%Eu3+ (cross 2) phosphors. The inset shows the corresponding luminescence photos (λex = 365 nm).
The CIE chromaticity coordinates of ZnGa2O4:xCDs,yEu3+ phosphors.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photoluminescence property and broad color emission of ZnGa2O4 phosphor due to the synergistic role of Eu3+ and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, P. O. Box 100, Beijing 100029, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: College of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, P. O. Box 100, Beijing 100029, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ZnGa2O4 phosphors co-composited with nanoscale carbon dots (CDs) and Eu3+ were presented for the tunable color emission. Novel single phase CDs or/and Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors were synthesized by microwave hydrothermal method and their optical properties were investigated. The ZnGa2O4 phosphors composited with CDs exhibited an intense broad blue light emission at 421 nm and a more enhanced photoluminescence intensity than those without CDs. The Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors gave an ideal red color emission. The CDs/Eu3+ co-composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors exhibited a wide emission band peak at 450 nm and narrow emission peak at 618 nm. Furthermore, the tunable color emissions of CDs/Eu3+ co-composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors from blue to the white light region, and then to red were obtained with the increasing Eu3+ concentration, which can be a promising single phased phosphor candidate in light emitting diodes. Broadly tunable emission single phased phosphor is tuned firstly through the synergistic role of the non-metal element and the rare earth metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Phosphor converted-white LEDs (pc-WLEDs) as a novel generation of solid-state light devices have been paid great attention due to their excellent properties, such as high brightness, high rendering index and environmental friendliness properties [1,2]. At present, two ways are mainly used to fabricate pc-WLEDs. One is to combine the blue diode chips and yellow phosphors. Another is to utilize ultraviolet (UV) light to excite Red/Green/Blue (RGB) tri-color phosphors. Due to the reabsorption of the blue emission by the green or red-emitting phosphors, luminous efficiency of the three-converter system is low. Therefore, great efforts have been made to search for one-phase and multiple color emission phosphors.

One-phase and multiple color emission phosphors can be obtained by doping or co-doping transition metal and rare earth metal ions, which are based on the energy transfer between two co-doping metals. A common way to obtain multiple color emission phosphor is the co-doping of Eu2+ or Eu3+ with other metal ions, such as Ba2Ca(B3O6)2:Eu2+,Mn2+ [3], CaCO3:Eu3+,Tb3+ [4] and Sr2MgSiO5:Eu2+,Mn2+ [5]. In order to obtain efficient phosphors, the coexistence of tetrahedral and octahedral cationic sites is the key factor for host materials. Zinc gallate (ZnGa2O4) belongs to a double oxide with a spinel structure, where Zn2+ ions are in the tetrahedral sites surrounded by four oxygen ions and Ga3+ ions are in the octahedral sites surrounded by six oxygen ions [6]. ZnGa2O4 can act as an excellent host material for high photoluminous and multicolor-emitting phosphors when composited with appropriate activators, like Mn-activated ZnGa2O4 (ZnGa2O4:Mn2+) for green emission [7] and Cr-activated ZnGa2O4 (ZnGa2O4:Cr3+) for red emission [8]. Tunable color emission can also be obtained when ZnGa2O4 is used as the host material. Broadly tunable emission from Mn-doped zinc gallogermanate phosphor has been synthesized through controlling the concentration of Ge [9]. The white light emission can be obtained by altering the doping concentration of metal ions in the triple doped sample of ZnGa2O4:Dy3+,Tb3+,Mn2+ [10]. Bi3+/Eu3+ co-doped ZnGa2O4 phosphor [11] has shown tunable color emission, which can be as a potential application in light emitting diodes. In brief, most of tunable color or white light emission emissions of ZnGa2O4 phosphors are adjusted by double doping or triple doping metal ions and rare earth metal ions [12,13].

Nanoscale carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention for a wide variety of promising applications owing to their excellent properties, such as low toxicity, good compatibility and strong fluorescence. The CDs with the size of 1.2–3.8 nm have been prepared and their size-dependent photoluminescence (PL) has been observed, showing excellent up-conversion performance [14]. Carbon composited CdS nanoparticles have exhibited the enhanced photoluminescence properties [15]. Silica-based CDs/CdS nanocrystal hybrids with a bright white fluorescence under UV light have been obtained [16]. Therefore, much attention can be attracted to explore the novel application of CDs composited semiconductor materials in the tunable color emission of one-phase phosphors [17,18].

The synergistic role of non-metal material and metal ions in the emission of phosphors has scarcely been focused on. Thus, we expect to investigate the combined effect of carbon dots (CDs) and rare earth metal ion Eu3+ on the ZnGa2O4 phosphors. Herein, we present microwave hydrothermal synthesis of CDs or/and Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors and their photoluminescence properties. Nanosized CDs or Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors with the nominal formula of ZnGa2O4:xCDs or ZnGa2O4:yEu3+ were synthesized and characterized by scanning electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction and photoluminescence spectrophotometer. Their crystal structures and luminescence properties were investigated. Moreover, CDs and Eu3+ co-composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors were obtained and their multiple color emissions were studied through the analysis of photoluminescence spectra and the Commission Internationale de'IEclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates. The synergistic role of non-metal and rare earth metal (CDs and Eu3+) for the one-phase and tunable color emission ZnGa2O4 phosphor was discussed.

Water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots used in this experiment were prepared following the reported method [19]. Citric acid (3 g) and urea (3 g) were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water and then a transparent solution was formed. The solution was heated for 5 min at 750 W in a domestic microwave oven. During the process, the color of solution changed from colorless to brown and finally the solution changed into brown solid cluster, which demonstrated the formation of CDs. Subsequently, the solid was transferred into a vacuum dry oven for 1 h at 60 °C. CDs aqueous solution was centrifuged and purified to remove the large particles. The supernatant was then evaporated with the rotary evaporation apparatus and the final product was named as CDs and was dried in oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs or/and Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors
ce:section.ce:para: Phosphors with the nominal formula of ZnGa2O4:xCDs or ZnGa2O4:yEu3+ were synthesized via microwave hydrothermal synthesis method in the presence of sodium citrate. At first, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (1 mmol), Ga(NO3)3 (2 mmol) and Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (y mmol) were dissolved in 15 mL deionized water. A transparent solution was formed and transferred into a 35-mL vessel. The solution was kept at pH = 5 by adding appropriate sodium hydroxide solution continuously with vigorous stirring. Sodium citrate (1.5 mmol) was added into the above solution, and the molar ratio of total metal to sodium citrate was 2:1. The solution was stirred for 1 h, and then was treated at the fixed temperature 180 °C for 40 min in a microwave reactor (Discover SP, CEM Corporation, USA) operated at 100 W with dynamic mode. After being cooled to the room temperature naturally, the white slurry was collected and washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times. The obtained solid product was dried in the air for 12 h. The final white product was named as ZnGa2O4:yEu3+, in which y referred to the molar percentage of Eu3+ corresponding to the total molar amount of Zn2+ and Ga3+. ZnGa2O4:xCDs and ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,yEu3+ were synthesized following the same method mentioned above, where x and 4% represented the molar percentage of CDs relative to the total molar amount of Zn2+ and Ga3+. The previously obtained CDs were added in the starting solution of the mixtures including Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Ga(NO3)3 or/and Eu(NO3)3·6H2O. The in-situ syntheses of ZnGa2O4:xCDs and ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,yEu3+ were carried out with the presence of the CDs.

The crystallographic phases of the ZnGa2O4 samples were analyzed by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Germany Bruker AXS Ltd.). The step width and the time-per-step of the XRD analysis were 0.02° and 0.1 s, respectively. Morphologies of the obtained phosphor nanoparticles were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4700, Japan JEOL Ltd.). Elemental composition of the sample was determined by energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS, Genesis 60, U. S. EDAX). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1901, Beijing Persee General Instrument Co. Ltd.). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed in an ultrahigh vacuum VG ESCALAB 250 electron spectrometer equipped with a multichannel detector. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained by Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The CIE chromaticity coordinates (x, y) were calculated from the equidistant wavelength method based on the photoluminescence emission spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of ZnGa2O4 phosphors composited with CDs or/and Eu3+
ce:section.ce:para: Composited with the varying amount of CDs or/and Eu3+, several different ZnGa2O4 phosphors were synthesized by microwave hydrothermal method. The obtained ZnGa2O4, ZnGa2O4:4%CDs, ZnGa2O4:8%Eu3+, ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,8%Eu3+ phosphors were characterized by SEM and XRD. SEM images of them are shown in Fig. 1 . As shown in Fig. 1a and b, ZnGa2O4 and ZnGa2O4:4%CDs nanoparticles are uniform and well distributed, in which one particle with the size around 100 nm is composed of many small nanoparticles about 10–20 nm. However, the sizes and morphologies of ZnGa2O4:8%Eu3+ and ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,8%Eu3+ nanoparticles (Fig. 1c and d) are a little bit large and irregular, which may be due to the doping of Eu3+ into ZnGa2O4. Energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) mapping analysis of ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,8%Eu3+ confirms the presence of the C, O, Zn, Ga and Eu elements. The XRD patterns of ZnGa2O4, ZnGa2O4:4%CDs, ZnGa2O4:8%Eu3+ and ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,8%Eu3+ nanoparticles are depicted in Fig. 2 . Several broad peaks for the ZnGa2O4 sample are shown in Fig. 2a, which is due to small size of nanocrystals [20]. All diffraction peaks of ZnGa2O4 can be indexed to (111), (220), (311), (400), (511), (440), (533) planes for the standard data of ZnGa2O4 (JCPDS card No. 38-1240), revealing spinel phase ZnGa2O4 formed [21]. According to the Debye-Scherrer formula, the average size of the ZnGa2O4 calculated from the half width of the diffraction peak (2θ = 35.7°) is 75 nm. The diffraction peaks of the CDs-composited ZnGa2O4 (Fig. 2b) are similar to that of ZnGa2O4, indicating that the composited CDs have no effect on the crystal structure. The diffraction peaks in the curves c and d are broader than those in the curves a and b, which could be ascribed to the poor crystallinity because of high disorder in the host lattice resulting from the incorporation of the rare earth Eu3+ ions with larger ion radius. Due to the larger ionic radius of Eu3+ (1.13 Å) than Ga3+ (0.62 Å) and Zn2+ (0.74 Å) ions in the ZnGa2O4, the doping of Eu3+ in the host lattice of ZnGa2O4 may cause a high disorder with the ionic radius mismatch and thus the crystallinity may be decreased (as verified from XRD patterns). Because of the charge match of Eu3+ with Ga3+ [11], Eu3+ is more likely to occupy the Ga3+ sites than Zn2+ sites within ZnGa2O4. Therefore, it is not easy to tell about the nature of the Eu3+ doping in ZnGa2O4 spinel nanoparticles. Anyway, the introduced disorder may be caused by the ionic radius mismatch between Eu3+, and Zn2+ or Ga3+ [22].

X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) can give useful information on the composition and chemical state of nanomaterials. Fig. 3 shows XPS spectra of the ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,8%Eu3+ phosphor. The BEs of Ga 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 for ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,8%Eu3+ are 1145.9 and 1119.1 eV, respectively. And the BEs of Zn 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 are 1045.7 and 1022.6 eV, respectively. The energy separation between Zn 2p3/2 and Ga 2p3/2 peaks (ΔE) of the sample is calculated to be about 96.5 eV, which agrees well with the reference for the formation of spinel [23]. The BEs of Eu 3d are centered at 1126.3 and 1156.8 eV, respectively. Two functional groups like C=O (288.0 eV) and C-H/C-C (284.7 eV) are detected as shown in Fig. 3c and d. The above results suggest the successful compositing of Eu and CDs in the sample. In addition, the FTIR spectra of the ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,8%Eu3+ phosphor shown in Fig. 3d verify the groups C=O and CH2 from CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence properties of the CDs-composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors
ce:section.ce:para: For the syntheses of CDs-composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors, water-soluble CDs were obtained firstly. Luminescence properties of the CDs were studied. The UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra (PL) of CDs are given in Fig. S1. The UV–visible absorption spectrum (Fig. S1a) indicates that CDs have a broad absorption band ranging from 300 nm to 500 nm. CDs exhibit the strongest peak at 436 nm monitored at 540 nm shown in the excitation spectrum (Fig. S1b, red curve), which is coincident with the UV–visible absorption spectrum. The emission spectrum (Fig. S1b, black curve) shows a strongest peak at 540 nm under the excitation of 436 nm with a broad emission band. The photographs of the CDs in sunlight (left) and under a 365 nm UV lamp (right) are shown in Fig. S1c, which show brown yellow and an intense green emission, respectively.

The excitation and emission spectra of CDs-composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors with the different amount of CDs are shown in Fig. 4 . The excitation spectrum demonstrates that pure ZnGa2O4 phosphor without CDs exhibits the strong peak at 245 nm and rather weak peak at 342 nm monitored at 418 nm. The position of emission peak is at 418 nm shown in Fig. 4b with excitation at 342 nm, indicating a blue shift corresponding to the previous reports [20,24]. In general, the reduced particle size of phosphor and the corresponding quantum size effect can contribute to the distinct blue shift [25]. Thus, the blue shift of emission peak for the present pure ZnGa2O4 phosphor is reasonable. Moreover, for the ZnGa2O4 phosphors composited with different amount of CDs, intensity of excitation and emission peaks changes obviously as shown in Fig. 4, while the position of emission peak shifts from 418 nm to 421 nm upon the excitation at 366 nm. By calculation, the luminescence intensity of the CDs composited ZnGa2O4 is greatly enhanced comparing with pure ZnGa2O4 without CDs.

As reported by Kang et al. [26], Si doping effect has been found on luminescence property of Si-composited ZnGa2O4. Due to the doping of Si4+ in ZnGa2O4 phosphor, two kinds of the donor levels were created, resulting in the red shift of the emission peak and the luminous intensity enhancement. Besides, the literature [27,28] reported that the zinc vacancy (Vzn) and the oxygen vacancy (Vo) can result in the enhanced emission intensity. Here, the CDs obtained in advance are involved in the metal precursors solution before the formation of CDs-composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors. Therefore, the CDs are composited with ZnGa2O4, which may cause the vacancy and disorder of ZnGa2O4. The increased disorder and vacancy concentration may result from the addition of CDs, which is responsible for the luminescence enhancement of CDs-composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors.

Based on emission spectra of ZnGa2O4:xCDs (x = 0, 1.4, 4.0, 6.5 and 8.9 mol%) phosphors, CIE chromaticity coordinates are calculated and exhibited in Fig. S2 and Table S1. The corresponding luminescence photographs under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp are also shown in Fig. S2. Known from Table S1, the CIE chromaticity coordinates of ZnGa2O4 vary with the compositing of CDs, which is also evidenced clearly from the coordinates in Fig. S2. The photographs in Fig. S2 show that the luminance of ZnGa2O4 phosphors is enhanced gradually with the increasing CDs concentration and reaches to the highest intensity in the case of 6.5 mol% CDs composited, following the dramatical decrease with further increasing CDs concentration. The results match the change trend in Fig. 4b. What's more, the ZnGa2O4 phosphor with CDs concentration of 8.9 mol% irradiated at 365 nm becomes white, while the color of the sample is brown yellow in sunlight, which can be used as white LEDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence properties of the Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors
ce:section.ce:para:
Several Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors (ZnGa2O4:yEu3+) were synthesized with the varying amount of Eu3+ from y = 5%–11%, in which y refers to the molar percentage of Eu3+ corresponding to the total molar amount of Zn2+ and Ga3+. PL spectra of the obtained ZnGa2O4:yEu3+ phosphors shown in Fig. 5 a and b indicate the luminescence intensities, including excitation and emission, reach to a maximum value when the Eu3+ concentration is at 8 mol%. Clearly, Fig. 5c shows the concentration dependence of the emission intensity of Eu3+ 5D0-7F2 transitions in ZnGa2O4:yEu3+ phosphors. The emission intensity increases with the increasing Eu3+ concentration up to a maximum (8 mol%), then decreases with the further increasing Eu3+ concentration because of concentration quenching. Known from the Dexter's energy transfer theory, the non-radiative energy migration among the activators at high concentration mainly contributed to the concentration quenching due to the exchange interaction or multipole-multipole interaction [29]. As Aan Uiter reported, the emission intensity (I) per activator can be described as the following equation [29,30]:
(1)
I / m = k
[ 1 + β
( m )
θ / 3
]
− 1
in which m is Eu3+ concentration, k and β are constants for a given host in the same excitation condition, θ = 3, 6, 8, 10 represent the energy transfer among the nearest neighbour, the dipole-dipole, dipole-quadrupole, and quadrupole-quadrupole interactions, respectively. The linear relationship between log(I/m) and log(m) with a slope of (-θ/3) can be obtained and the dependence of log(I/m) on log(m) is shown in Fig. 5d. According to the value of the slope, θ is calculated to be 5.579, which is located between 3 and 6. Therefore, the concentration quenching of the Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors above is caused by energy transfer among the nearest neighbour and the dipole-dipole interaction.

The CIE chromaticity coordinates of ZnGa2O4:yEu3+(y = 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 and 11 mol%) phosphors are (0.64, 0.36), (0.64, 0.36), (0.64, 0.36), (0.65, 0.35), (0.64, 0.35) and (0.64, 0.36), respectively. All the chromaticity coordinates are more close to Commission Internationale de’IEclairage (CIE) color coordinates (x = 0.67, y = 0.33) of red color than that of ZnGa2O4:Eu3+ (0.457, 0.268) prepared by hydrothermal approach [13]. Therefore, the present Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors may have a commercialized potential as red phosphors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence properties of the Eu3+/CDs composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescence properties are compared between ZnGa2O4:4%CDs and ZnGa2O4:8%Eu3+ phosphors. As shown in Fig. 6 a, excitation spectrum of ZnGa2O4:4%CDs monitored at 421 nm shows a weak peak at about 245 nm and a strong peak at 366 nm, which originate from the octahedral Ga-O groups in the spinel lattice. The emission band peak at 421 nm originates from the self-activation center of octahedral Ga-O groups in the spinel lattice of ZnGa2O4:4%CDs [31]. In Fig. 6b, the excitation spectrum of ZnGa2O4:8%Eu3+ recorded by monitoring at 618 nm shows a broad band and sharp peaks in the range of 200 nm–550 nm, which are attributed to the 4f-4f transitions within the Eu3+ 4f electron configuration. A series of line emissions of ZnGa2O4:8%Eu3+ at 578 nm, 595 nm, 618 nm, 653 nm and 702 nm under the excitation of 395 nm are associated with f-f transition of Eu3+ corresponding to 5D0-7F0, 5D0-7F1, 5D0-7F2, 5D0-7F3 and 5D0-7F4 respectively. A significant spectra overlap can be observed by comparing the excitation band of ZnGa2O4:8%Eu3+ with the emission band of ZnGa2O4:4%CDs, which may contribute to energy transfer from host to Eu3+. Therefore, the phosphors through the subsequent doping of Eu3+ in ZnGa2O4:4%CDs are synthesized and discussed.

In order to study the effect of doping concentration of Eu3+ ions on the photoluminescence properties of the phosphors, several ZnGa2O4:4%CDs, yEu3+ (y = 1, 3, 6, 7, 8, 9 and 11 mol%) samples have been synthesized and characterized by fluorescence spectrophotometer. As shown in Fig. 7 , the excitation spectra of the ZnGa2O4:4%CDs, yEu3+ samples monitored at 618 nm show a strong and broad excitation band of CDs-composited ZnGa2O4, which may be due to the energy transfer of host to Eu3+. A broad band of 200–388 nm shown in Fig. 7a is ascribed to the charge transfer band (CTB) of Eu3+-O2- together with absorption of ZnGa2O4 host lattice [32,33]. A number of line excitation peaks in the excitation spectra is assigned to the f-f transition of Eu3+ [34]. Fig. 7b reveals the emission spectra of ZnGa2O4:4%CDs, yEu3+ at the excitation of 395 nm. Interestingly, a broad band emission peak around 450 nm appears in the emission spectra of ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,yEu3+, exhibiting a red shift compared with that of ZnGa2O4:xCDs in Fig. 4. The emission peak shifting to longer wavelength may be ascribed to the enhancement of the crystal field surrounding due to the composited carbon. The disorder of ZnGa2O4 causes the shrinkage of the cell volume and the length will become shorter, which leads to the emission of ZnGa2O4 shifting to the longer wavelength. Fig. 7b also shows the series of line emissions of Eu3+. The highest emission peak at 618 nm is attributed to the electron dipole transition of Eu3+ due to the lack of inversion symmetry sites, which indicates that Eu3+ ions locates at symmetry sites [35]. Moreover, the emission intensity of ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,yEu3+ phosphors strongly depends on Eu3+ concentration. The blue emission at 450 nm decreases and the red emission at 618 nm increases with the increasing of Eu3+ concentration, suggesting the possible energy transfer from host to Eu3+. When the concentration of Eu3+ reaches to a value of y = 8%, the emission intensity at 618 nm also decreases with the further increasing of Eu3+ concentration because of the concentration quenching effect.

To further study the synergistic role between CDs and Eu3+ in the ZnGa2O4 host, a series of contrast experiments were carried out in the following work. The physical mixtures of CDs and ZnGa2O4 nanoparticles, CDs and Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4, Eu3+ and CDs composited ZnGa2O4 as well as Eu3+ and ZnGa2O4 were prepared by simply mixing the corresponding materials. PL spectra of the physical mixtures are shown in Fig. 8 . It can be seen that the physical mixtures of CDs and ZnGa2O4 nanoparticles, CDs and Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 nanoparticles show only strong CDs emission and no Eu3+ excitation at 395 nm shown in Fig. 8a and b, respectively. This indicates that the presence of CDs in the ZnGa2O4 matrix is important to sensitize Eu3+ emission. Furthermore, the simple physical mixtures containing Eu3+ and CDs composited ZnGa2O4 or ZnGa2O4 show weak CDs emission and Eu3+ emission depicted in Fig. 8c and d, respectively. All these results imply the importance of the co-composited CDs and Eu3+ in a suitable matrix and a synergistic role between CDs and Eu3+ in the ZnGa2O4 host.

Fig. 9 shows decay curves of ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,y%Eu3+ (y = 7, 8, 9 and 11 mol%) phosphors (λex = 395 nm, λem = 618 nm). The luminescence decay curves of Eu3+ (5D0-7F2) are well fitted to double exponential function as the following equation [36]:
(2)
I =
A 1
exp
( − t /
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
( − t /
τ 2
)
+ B
where I is the emission intensity at time t; A1, A2 are constants; τ1, τ2 are the double exponential components of the decay time. B is the value for different fitting. The fluorescence lifetimes calculated by the simulated parameters mentioned above are 1.610 ms, 1.903 ms, 1.990 ms and 3.1777 ms, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime increases with increasing Eu3+ concentration, suggesting the existence of energy transfer from host to Eu3+.

The CIE chromaticity coordinates of the ZnGa2O4:4%CDs, ZnGa2O4:4%CDs,yEu3+ (y = 1, 3, 8 and 11 mol%) and ZnGa2O4:8%Eu3+ phosphors under the excitation of 395 nm are calculated based on the emission spectra, which match with the output wavelength of ultraviolet light and blue light LED chip. The values of the CIE chromaticity coordinates are presented in Table 1 . Fig. 10 depicts that the emission color of the phosphors gradually changes from blue to the white light region, and then to red with the increasing Eu3+ concentration, which is also demonstrated by the changing CIE chromaticity coordinates. The luminescence photographs of different samples corresponding to the CIE chromaticity coordinates under the excitation of ultraviolet lamp at 365 nm are also shown in the inset (Fig. 10). The above results indicate that the tunable color emission can be obtained by changing the concentrations of CDs and Eu3+. There exists a synergistic role between non-metal CDs and rare earth metal Eu3+. Therefore, they can be as a kind of promising single phased phosphor candidate in phosphor converted WLEDs, considering the superior emission characteristics of CDs or/and Eu3+ composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors.

Many ZnGa2O4 phosphors are synthesized by microwave hydrothermal method in the presence of sodium citrate. The CDs composited ZnGa2O4 phosphor exhibits an intense broad blue light emission at 421 nm. The photoluminescence intensity of ZnGa2O4 composited with CDs (6.5 mol%) is much enhanced and higher than that of pure ZnGa2O4. Eu3+ (8 mol%) composited ZnGa2O4 has the chromaticity coordinate (x = 0.65, y = 0.35) which is closed to the ideal red color standard CIE coordinates (x = 0.67, y = 0.33) regulated by National Television System Committee. A certain amount of CDs and different amount of Eu3+ co-composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors are synthesized and a synergistic role of non-metal CDs and rare earth ions Eu3+ are found. Broadly tunable color emissions from blue to the white region, and then to red are obtained with the increasing Eu3+ concentration in CDs/Eu3+ co-composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors. The color tunable CDs/Eu3+ co-composited ZnGa2O4 phosphors could be promising single phased phosphor candidates in phosphor converted WLEDs, which are potential for the application in light emitting diodes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21106006) is acknowledged greatly.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2017.06.013.
